beats.prompt: Warm up: match each idea to its answer.

pairs.left: Penney's game: the single best pattern
A move that's best no matter what others do
Value of a 50/50 bet paying +2 or 0

feedback.correct: Right. Today: when one move beats every alternative regardless of the opponent — and what happens when both players have one.
hints: Penney's game had no single best pattern — every pattern is beaten by another.
A strategy that wins whatever the opponent does is 'dominant'.
Match: no best pattern, dominant strategy, and E = ½·2 + ½·0 = 1.

beats.prompt: Two suspects are questioned separately. Each may stay silent (Cooperate) or confess (Defect). Payoffs (higher = better): both silent (3,3); both confess (1,1); if one confesses while the other stays silent, the confessor gets 5 and the silent one 0. Both are perfectly rational. What happens?

Both stay silent → (3,3).note: Tempting, but unstable: whatever the other does, confessing pays more (5>3 and 1>0). Each has a private incentive to break the pact.

Both confess → (1,1).note: Yes — confessing strictly dominates staying silent for both, so both confess and land at (1,1), worse than the (3,3) they could have shared.

One confesses, one stays silent.note: Not stable either: the silent player would switch to confessing (0 → 1). Both end up confessing.

interaction.title: Strict dominance
interaction.body: Strategy X strictly dominates strategy Y for a player if X gives that player a strictly higher payoff than Y against EVERY choice the opponent could make. A rational player never plays a strictly dominated strategy — and if one strategy dominates all the others, it's a dominant strategy: just play it.

feedback.correct: Keep that test in mind: compare a strategy's payoff column-by-column against the alternative.
hints: 'Strictly' means greater in every column, with no ties.
If a strategy is dominated, cross it out — you'll never play it.
Read and tap Continue.

beats.prompt: Here is the Prisoner's Dilemma as a payoff grid (row player, column player). Tap a row to test whether it's dominated. Which strategy should each rational player pick?

feedback.correct: Defect strictly dominates Cooperate in BOTH columns (5>3 and 1>0), for both players. So both defect — the only stable cell is (Defect, Defect) = (1,1).
hints: Compare the Cooperate row to the Defect row, column by column.
Defect pays 5 vs 3 (if the other cooperates) and 1 vs 0 (if the other defects) — higher in both.
A dominated strategy is never played: both defect → (1,1).

hero.structuralReadout: Defect out-pays Cooperate in every column → both defect → the (1,1) cell.

beats.prompt: Same game, different lens: tap a column to highlight the row player's best response to it. Notice both arrows point to Defect.

feedback.correct: Whatever column you pick, the row player's best response is Defect — that's exactly what 'dominant' means.
hints: Tap the Cooperate column: the bigger row payoff is 5 (Defect).
Tap the Defect column: the bigger row payoff is 1 (Defect).
Best response is Defect in both columns → dominant.

beats.prompt: Dominance can cascade. In this 3×3 game, repeatedly eliminate strictly dominated strategies (tap a column or row to test it, or use Solve). What single cell survives?

feedback.correct: Round 1: Right is dominated for Column (Center beats it everywhere) → gone. Round 2: with Right gone, Middle dominates Top and Bottom for Row → gone. Round 3: Column now prefers Left to Center. The survivor is (Middle, Left).
hints: Start with Column's options: Center's payoffs (1,1,5) beat Right's (0,0,0) in every row — drop Right.
Now Row compares over Left/Center only: Middle (2,3) beats Top (0,1) and Bottom (1,1) — drop Top and Bottom.
Finally Column picks Left over Center in the Middle row (3>1). Survivor: (Middle, Left).

hero.structuralReadout: Eliminate Right, then Top & Bottom, then Center — one cell survives: (Middle, Left).

beats.prompt: Three ways to read the Prisoner's Dilemma. Predict the stable outcome, then reveal each lens.

lenses.label: Dominant strategy
lenses.body: Defecting out-pays cooperating against every opponent choice — so each player defects on their own.

lenses.label: Nash equilibrium
lenses.body: At (Defect, Defect) neither player can do better by switching alone — it is self-enforcing.

lenses.label: Pareto comparison
lenses.body: (Cooperate, Cooperate) = (3,3) is better for BOTH, yet it is not an equilibrium: each is tempted to defect to 5.

feedback.correct: Dominance forces it, Nash confirms it's stable, and Pareto shows the tragedy: rationality lands both players below the cooperative outcome.
hints: Dominant: best regardless of the opponent.
Nash: no one gains by deviating alone.
Pareto: a better-for-both outcome exists, but it isn't stable.

beats.interviewNote: The 'guess 2/3 of the average' game (Keynes's beauty contest) is a classic desk question and a market metaphor: iterated dominance drives the unique equilibrium to 0, but real players (and traders) reason only a few levels deep, so winning guesses cluster around 20–35. The lesson is that 'rational' equilibrium and observed behavior can diverge — pricing what others will do, not just fundamentals.
beats.prompt: Everyone picks a real number in [0, 100]; the winner is closest to 2/3 of the average of all picks. By iterated elimination of dominated guesses, what is the unique Nash-equilibrium guess?

feedback.correct: 0. No rational player picks above 2/3·100 ≈ 66.7; given that, none picks above 2/3·66.7 ≈ 44.4; iterating this collapses every guess to 0 — the only fixed point.
hints: The target is 2/3 of the average, so guesses above 2/3·100 are dominated.
Eliminate those, and now guesses above 2/3 of the new max are dominated — repeat.
The chain of eliminations converges to a single value: 0.

beats.prompt: Lock it in with the Prisoner's Dilemma below.

interaction.scenario: In the Prisoner's Dilemma both players' only dominant strategy is to Defect, so the unique Nash equilibrium is \((\text{Defect}, \text{Defect})\). Payoffs: \((C,C)=(3,3)\), \((D,D)=(1,1)\), and a lone defector earns \(5\) while the cooperator earns \(0\).

fields.label: Payoff to EACH player at the Nash equilibrium
Payoff to each if both had cooperated

feedback.correct: At the equilibrium both Defect, so each earns \(1\). Had both Cooperated they'd each earn \(3\) — Pareto-superior, but not an equilibrium because either could deviate to \(5\). \[ (D,D)=(1,1) \quad\text{vs}\quad (C,C)=(3,3). \]
hints: The equilibrium cell is (Defect, Defect).
Read that cell's payoff: both get 1.
The cooperative cell (Cooperate, Cooperate) pays 3 each.

feedback.correct: A strictly dominant strategy beats every alternative — play it. When both players have one, you can be trapped below the cooperative outcome (the Prisoner's Dilemma). Next: when no one has a dominant strategy, how do we find the stable outcome? → Nash equilibrium (L2).
hints: Dominance: best regardless of the opponent.
Iterated dominance can solve bigger games (and the 2/3 game).
Bridge: L2 — equilibrium when nothing is dominant.
